title: Hand on Belly, Hand on Heart, Three Rounds of Breath
subtitle: A three-minute body practice for when the day has pulled you out of yourself
excerpt: A three-minute embodiment practice. One hand on the belly, one hand on the heart, three rounds of slow breath. Used to bring attention back into the body when the head has been running the day.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Hand-on-belly hand-on-heart body anchors appear across body-based mindfulness, somatic experiencing work, and self-compassion practice (the form used here is closest to the standard self-compassion break taught in Kristin Neff's body-based work, applied here without the verbal component).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the day has been in the head for hours and you can feel the body has been forgotten. Also good before a meeting, after a difficult conversation, or as a reset between two parts of the day.

content.text: A three-minute embodiment practice. One hand on the belly, one hand on the heart, three rounds of slow breath. The practice brings attention out of the head and back into the body.
New to embodiment as a practice? Read
Sit or stand somewhere you will not be interrupted for three minutes. The feet are on the floor. The shoulders can drop.
Place one hand flat on the belly, just below the navel. Place the other hand flat on the heart, over the centre of the chest. The hands stay there for the whole practice.

Round one. Breathe in slowly through the nose, letting the belly rise under the lower hand. Pause at the top. Breathe out through the mouth, letting the belly drop. The hand on the heart stays steady.
Round two. The same shape. Breathe in, belly rises. Pause. Breathe out, belly settles. On the out-breath, let the shoulders drop another inch if they have crept up.
Round three. The same shape, slower if it is available. Breathe in, belly rises. Pause. Breathe out, belly settles. The third out-breath is the long one.
Keep the hands in place for one more breath. The hand on the belly knows you are breathing. The hand on the heart knows you are here. Then let them go and carry on with the day.

Original to homemade.education. The hand-on-belly hand-on-heart shape is used in body-based mindfulness, in somatic work, and in self-compassion teaching. The version here uses breath only, so the practice fits a three-minute window.
